headline: Ancient Water Trick Brings Life Back to Parched Border Ranches
meta_description: Ranchers on the US-Mexico border are using ancient rock structures called trincheras to combat drought and restore degraded grasslands.
summary_short: On Sonora's struggling ranches, centuries-old rock barriers are slowing water and bringing back life to drought-ravaged land.
body:
Eduardo Ríos Colores pointed across his Sonoran ranch toward a bone-dry streambed cutting through brown grass. "There used to be a lot of trees," he said. "But the trees have been drying up."

Like many ranchers in northwestern Mexico, Ríos Colores has watched his landscape deteriorate under relentless drought. In 2025 alone, he sold more than three-quarters of his herd because there was simply no grass to feed them. When rain does come, it arrives violently—in 2022, monsoon downpours dumped 85 inches on the region in a single season, three times what the land needs, carving deep erosion channels across the terrain.

But a solution is taking root. Hundreds of small rock structures called trincheras now pepper Ríos Colores's streambed, each composed of fist-sized stones stacked to create permeable barriers. Built in 2021, these ancient water-slowing devices—used for thousands of years in the region—raise the water table and spread rainfall across the landscape. Rich soil gradually accumulates behind the structures, allowing native grasses to return and wildlife to flourish.

"The current flows like a snake," Ríos Colores explained, describing how the trincheras redirect destructive water flow.

The approach represents a rare meeting point between ranchers and conservationists. Organizations like the Sky Island Alliance, which protects the border-spanning Madrean Sky Islands ecoregion, are now collaborating with local producers to implement these regenerative practices—offering hope that degraded rangelands can heal.
